chunk_summary: Zinc deficiency in vegetarian-fed children arises from low bioavailability and phytate interference. Testing should consider clinical context and inflammation markers. Zinc supplementation is standard in acute diarrhea but not routinely recommended otherwise; dietary correction is preferred.
learning_objectives: Understand the mechanisms of zinc deficiency in vegetarian-fed children.
Learn how to interpret zinc test results in the context of clinical and inflammatory markers.
Identify appropriate scenarios for zinc supplementation and dietary correction strategies.
Recognize the role of zinc in managing acute diarrhea in children.
key_facts: Zinc deficiency in vegetarian-fed children is linked to low bioavailability and phytate-rich diets.
Serum/plasma zinc levels are influenced by inflammation, fasting, and sample handling.
In acute diarrhea, zinc supplementation is 10 mg/day for children <6 months and 20 mg/day for children ≥6 months for 14 days.
Routine long-term zinc supplementation is not recommended for vegetarian-fed children; dietary correction is prioritized.
Short therapeutic zinc trials may be considered for undernourished children with persistent poor appetite or recurrent diarrhea.
reference_quote: "In acute diarrhea, zinc is standard of care (10 mg/day for <6 months and 20 mg/day for ≥6 months for 14 days) alongside ORS and continued feeding." (WHO Clinical Management of Acute Diarrhoea)
document_key_topics: Zinc deficiency mechanisms in vegetarian-fed children
Zinc supplementation guidelines in acute diarrhea
